one_line_takeaway: When evaluated on a rigorously labeled, de-duplicated, realistically split benchmark (PRIMEVUL) with practice-relevant metrics (VD-S, pair-wise), state-of-the-art code LMs — including GPT-4 — detect real-world vulnerabilities no better than random guessing, meaning prior benchmark successes were largely artifacts of noisy labels, data leakage, and misleading metrics.
key_finding: Existing benchmarks vastly overestimate code-LM VD ability. StarCoder2 scores 68.26% F1 on BigVul but only 3.09% F1 on PRIMEVUL (train-BV/test-PV); even fine-tuned on PRIMEVUL it reaches just 18.05% F1. Label accuracy of vulnerable functions is very low in automatically labeled sets (BigVul 25%, CrossVul 47.8%, CVEfixes 51.7%, DiverseVul 60%) and even in some 'manually' labeled ones (CodeXGLUE/Devign 24%, VulnPatchPairs 36%), vs SVEN 94%; PRIMEVUL's own labeling reaches 86% (ONEFUNC) and 92% (NVDCHECK). Up to 18.9% of test samples are duplicated/leaked from training. VD-S exposes the mirage: CodeBERT looks strong on BigVul (F1 62.88%) yet has an 81.77% false-negative rate under VD-S, i.e. useless in practice. In pair-wise evaluation, GPT-4 with chain-of-thought reaches only 12.94% correct-pair (P-C), below the random-guess baseline of 22.70% P-C; GPT-3.5 CoT reaches 6.21% P-C, and fine-tuning GPT-3.5 makes it worse (1.24% P-C). Class weights and contrastive learning give only marginal gains.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is threefold: (1) an empirical audit exposing severe flaws (noisy labels, high duplication/leakage, misleading metrics) in existing code-LM vulnerability-detection (VD) benchmarks; (2) PRIMEVUL, a new C/C++ VD dataset (6,968 vulnerable + 228,800 benign functions across 755 projects, 6,827 commits, 140 CWEs) built with two novel automated labeling techniques — PRIMEVUL-ONEFUNC (uses commits touching a single function) and PRIMEVUL-NVDCHECK (uses expert CVE/NVD analysis) — plus rigorous de-duplication and chronological splitting; and (3) new evaluation guidelines: the Vulnerability Detection Score (VD-S = false-negative rate at a fixed false-positive rate, e.g. FPR ≤0.5%) and a pair-wise evaluation (P-C/P-V/P-B/P-R) that tests whether a model can distinguish a vulnerable function from its fixed benign counterpart. The overall finding is that code LMs perform near-random at realistic VD, far below what prior benchmarks suggested. (BigVul, SVEN, CodeXGLUE/Devign, DiverseVul, CVEfixes, CrossVul, VulnPatchPairs are cited prior datasets, NOT this paper's — PRIMEVUL is the paper's dataset.)
threat_model: This is a defensive/evaluation security paper, not an attack paper. The setting: defenders (security teams) want to use code language models to automatically flag security vulnerabilities in real-world C/C++ source; the practical constraint is that vulnerabilities are rare, so a usable detector must keep false positives extremely low (FPR ≤0.5%) while catching true vulnerabilities. The paper's 'threat' framing is the risk of over-trusting benchmark-validated detectors: models that look accurate under standard metrics have false-negative rates around 80%+ and cannot distinguish a vulnerable function from its patched version, so deploying them would give a false sense of security. No adversarial attacker is modeled.
limitations: Scope is limited to C/C++ function-level VD; findings may not transfer to other languages or repository/inter-procedural granularity (the authors note vulnerabilities spanning multiple functions — race conditions, DoS, command injection — are hard to label at function level). GPT evaluation is constrained by cost/budget: GPT-4 fine-tuning was unavailable and only subsets/limited settings were used, and GPT-3.5 was fine-tuned for only one epoch. PRIMEVUL's own labeling, while high-accuracy (86–92%), is still automated and imperfect, and its 1:~33 vulnerable:benign ratio, though more realistic, can bias fine-tuned models toward shortcuts. The remedies explored (class weights, contrastive learning) are limited in number, so the negative result does not preclude other training approaches.

datasets_used: PRIMEVUL (introduced by this paper: 6,968 vulnerable + 228,800 benign functions, 755 projects, 6,827 commits, 140 CWEs)
SVEN (1,606 functions, 9 CWEs; manual-label accuracy reference)
quantitative_results: StarCoder2: 68.26% F1 on BigVul vs 3.09% F1 on PRIMEVUL (train-BigVul/test-PRIMEVUL); 18.05% F1 when trained on PRIMEVUL

Label accuracy of vulnerable functions: SVEN 94.0%, CodeXGLUE 24.0%, VulnPatchPairs 36.0%, BigVul 25.0%, CrossVul 47.8%, CVEfixes 51.7%, DiverseVul 60.0%, PRIMEVUL-ONEFUNC 86.0%, PRIMEVUL-NVDCHECK 92.0%
Data leakage: up to 18.9% of test samples are exact copies leaked from the train set
VD-S defined as FNR @ FPR ≤0.5%; CodeBERT on BigVul has F1 62.88% but 81.77% false-negative rate under VD-S
Pair-wise (P-C, higher better): GPT-4 CoT 12.94%, GPT-4 two-shot 5.14%, GPT-3.5 CoT 6.21%, GPT-3.5 two-shot 5.67%, GPT-3.5 fine-tune 1.24%, vs RANDOM GUESS 22.70%
PRIMEVUL covers 140 CWEs = 15.6x SVEN, and 16.7x as many vulnerable C/C++ functions as SVEN
baselines_compared: Prior benchmark BigVul (as the overestimating baseline)
Training remedies: class weights and contrastive learning (incl. CA-CLR)
